provisions.title: Group 1 Cryptoassets: Tokenised Traditional Assets
provisions.text: Group 1a cryptoassets are tokenised traditional assets (for example, tokenised bonds, equities, or commodities) that meet classification conditions. These assets receive the same capital treatment as the underlying traditional asset. Group 1b cryptoassets are cryptoassets with effective stabilisation mechanisms (stablecoins backed by traditional assets) that meet redemption risk and basis risk tests. Group 1b assets receive a capital add-on but are otherwise treated as their underlying reserve assets. Banks must continuously monitor whether Group 1 classification conditions are met.

provisions.title: Group 2 Cryptoassets: Unbacked and Non-Qualifying Stablecoins
provisions.text: Group 2 cryptoassets include: Group 2a (cryptoassets that meet a set of hedging recognition criteria and can apply a modified market risk approach); and Group 2b (all other cryptoassets, including Bitcoin and Ether, which receive a 1250% risk weight). The 1250% risk weight for Group 2b effectively requires banks to hold capital equal to the full value of the exposure. Banks' aggregate exposure to Group 2 cryptoassets should generally be below 2% of Tier 1 capital, with a hard limit of 5%.

provisions.title: Cryptoasset Infrastructure Risk Add-On
provisions.text: All cryptoasset exposures are subject to an infrastructure risk add-on to capture risks specific to distributed ledger technology and blockchain infrastructure. These risks include: smart contract risk (coding errors, vulnerabilities); settlement finality risk (finality uncertainty, chain reorganisations); operational risk of DLT networks (consensus mechanism failures, 51% attacks); and governance risk (lack of centralised governance, hard forks). The infrastructure risk add-on is set at 2.5% of the exposure for Group 1 cryptoassets.

provisions.title: Cryptoasset Disclosure Requirements
provisions.text: Banks with cryptoasset exposures must disclose: the classification of cryptoasset exposures (Group 1a, 1b, 2a, 2b); the notional amount and risk-weighted assets for each group; the accounting classification and measurement approach; the nature and extent of cryptoasset activities (trading, custody, lending, staking); exposure to DeFi protocols; the infrastructure risk add-on; and qualitative information on risk management practices for cryptoasset exposures, including policies on permissioned vs. permissionless blockchains.

provisions.title: Principles for Effective Management of Climate-Related Financial Risks
provisions.text: The Basel Committee has published 18 principles for the effective management and supervision of climate-related financial risks. Principles 1-12 address bank practices: corporate governance (Principle 1: board and senior management responsibility); internal control framework (Principle 2: policies and procedures); capital and liquidity adequacy (Principle 3: incorporation into ICAAP and ILAAP); risk management process (Principle 4: integration into risk management framework); management monitoring and reporting (Principle 5: data aggregation and reporting); credit risk management (Principle 6: climate risk in credit risk assessment); market risk management (Principle 7: climate risk impact on trading book); liquidity risk management (Principle 8: climate risk impact on liquidity); operational risk management (Principle 9: climate risk impact on operations); and scenario analysis (Principle 10: climate scenario analysis).

provisions.title: Supervisory Principles for Climate Risk
provisions.text: The Basel Committee's supervisory principles for climate risk (Principles 13-18) address: supervisory responsibilities (Principle 13: prudential regulatory and supervisory requirements); supervisory review process (Principle 14: incorporation into Pillar 2); supervisory tools (Principle 15: appropriate supervisory tools including stress testing); supervisory cooperation (Principle 16: international cooperation on climate risk supervision); supervisory reporting (Principle 17: appropriate data collection); and supervisory approach (Principle 18: proportionality and risk-based supervision of climate risk).

provisions.title: Revised Guidelines on Sound Management of AML/CFT Risks
provisions.text: The Basel Committee's revised guidelines on sound management of risks related to money laundering and financing of terrorism cover: the role of supervisors in assessing banks' AML/CFT risk management; consolidated AML/CFT risk management across banking groups; the interaction between AML/CFT supervision and prudential supervision; customer acceptance policies; customer identification and ongoing monitoring; suspicious transaction reporting; and specific risk areas (correspondent banking, payment services, trade finance). The guidelines emphasise that ML/TF risk is a component of operational risk that should be integrated into banks' overall risk management framework.

provisions.title: AML/CFT in Correspondent Banking
provisions.text: The Basel Committee has published guidance on AML/CFT due diligence in correspondent banking relationships. Key requirements include: risk-based due diligence on respondent banks (ownership structure, reputation, regulatory environment, AML/CFT controls); assessment of the respondent's customer base and services offered; prohibition of payable-through accounts without adequate controls; enhanced due diligence for higher-risk correspondent relationships; monitoring of transactions processed through correspondent accounts; and periodic review and update of due diligence. The guidelines also address de-risking concerns, noting that the wholesale termination of correspondent banking relationships is not a risk-based approach.

provisions.title: Principles for Operational Resilience
provisions.text: The Basel Committee's principles for operational resilience (March 2021) require banks to: identify and map critical operations (the operations which, if disrupted, would pose a threat to the bank's viability or financial stability); set impact tolerances (the maximum tolerable disruption for each critical operation); design and test scenarios (severe but plausible disruption scenarios, including cyber attacks, natural disasters, and third-party failures); invest in protective technology and processes; and develop recovery capabilities to resume critical operations within impact tolerances. The principles apply on a proportionate basis to the nature, scale, complexity, and risk profile of the bank.

provisions.title: Mapping Interconnections and Dependencies
provisions.text: Banks should map the interconnections and interdependencies (people, processes, technology, information, facilities, and third-party services) that support their critical operations. The mapping should identify: internal dependencies (shared services, technology platforms, key personnel); external dependencies (third-party service providers, financial market infrastructure, utilities); cross-border dependencies (operations in different jurisdictions, reliance on foreign service providers); and concentration risks (multiple critical operations dependent on the same third party or technology platform). Banks should use this mapping to identify vulnerabilities and design appropriate mitigation measures.

provisions.title: Board Composition and Qualifications
provisions.text: The board should have an appropriate number and mix of individuals to carry out its overall responsibilities. Board members should: be and remain qualified for their positions, individually and collectively; understand their oversight and governance role; exercise sound and independent judgement; have adequate knowledge, skills, and experience (including understanding of risks, regulatory environment, and financial products); ensure adequate time commitment; and complete appropriate induction and ongoing training. The board should include a sufficient number of independent directors to provide effective oversight.

provisions.title: Risk Committee and CRO
provisions.text: Banks should establish a risk committee (or equivalent) at the board level. The risk committee should: be composed of independent, non-executive directors; have a clearly defined mandate covering all material risk types; receive adequate information on the bank's risk profile; challenge and review management's risk proposals; and oversee the chief risk officer (CRO). The CRO should: report directly to the board risk committee (or CEO with dotted line to board); have sufficient stature, authority, and resources; be independent from business lines; and have the authority to escalate risk issues directly to the board.

provisions.title: Intraday Liquidity Monitoring Tools
provisions.text: The Basel Committee has published monitoring tools for intraday liquidity management. Banks should monitor: daily maximum intraday liquidity usage (the peak amount of intraday credit used during the day); available intraday liquidity at the start of the business day; total payments made and received (and their timing); time-specific and other critical obligations (payments that must be made at specific times); and the value of customer payments made on behalf of financial institution customers. These monitoring tools help supervisors assess banks' intraday liquidity management and their potential impact on payment systems.

provisions.title: Treatment of Deposit Insurance in LCR
provisions.text: For the purpose of the LCR calculation, retail deposits are divided into stable and less stable deposits based on the presence of deposit insurance. Stable retail deposits (fully covered by an effective deposit insurance scheme that meets the IADI Core Principles) receive a run-off rate of 5%. Less stable retail deposits (not covered or only partially covered by deposit insurance) receive a run-off rate of at least 10%. Jurisdictions may set higher run-off rates for less stable deposits based on their assessment of depositor behaviour during stress. The quality and effectiveness of the deposit insurance scheme is assessed against specified criteria.
